title: Conjecture 7
statement_text: We have that $T(n) = \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{n})$. More generally, we have that $T(G) = \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{|G|})$.
context_text: The paper defines $\widetilde{\Theta}(g(n))$ to mean $f = \Omega(g(n)(\log n)^a)$ and $f(n) = O(g(n)(\log n)^b)$ for some integers $a,b$ (i.e., equality up to polylogarithmic factors). Theorem 1 gives a lower bound $T(n) \geq n^{1/3}/(2(\log n/\log 2)^{2/3})$ and Theorem 3 gives $T(n) = O(n^{3/4+\varepsilon})$; the conjecture proposes the true growth rate is $\widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{n})$.
notes: PDF source — the tilde in $\widetilde{\Theta}$ is garbled in extraction (appears as bare 'e' or dropped); the argument of the second $\widetilde{\Theta}$ (presumably $\sqrt{|G|}$) is also lost in the PDF rendering. Statement reconstructed from context.
paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements

title: Question 8
statement_text: Is the behavior of $T(n)$ different when $n$ is prime? How much does $T(G)$ depend on the structure of $G$ in addition to the order of $G$?
context_text: The best upper bound constructions for $T(n)$ rely on the existence of subgroups of certain orders, so the best known upper bound for $T(p)$ when $p$ is prime is only $O(p^{3/4+\varepsilon})$, which may not be tight.
paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements

title: Question 9
statement_text: What is the optimal function in Theorem 5?
context_text: Theorem 5 shows that every non-empty solid subset of a finite abelian group $G$ of size $O((|G|/\log|G|)^{1/4})$ is a maximal supplement, but it is unclear whether this bound is anywhere near optimal.
paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements

title: Question 10
statement_text: How does the probability that a random subset of a finite group $G$ with parameter $p = p(|G|)$ is a minimal complement (or maximal supplement) vary with $p$? Is there a (sharp) threshold?
context_text: Although being a minimal complement is not a monotone property, Theorems 1 and 3 together show that for small $p$ a random set is with high probability a minimal complement, and for large $p$ it is with high probability not a minimal complement, suggesting threshold behavior.
paper_title: Inverse problems for minimal complements and maximal supplements
